data.title: Stocks fall as Trump's Tuesday night deadline for Iran looms: 'The market is certainly on edge'
data.description:
By Joy Wiltermuth and Frances Yue

U.S. stocks were lower Tuesday - but with the S&P 500 still positive in April - as Trump intensified his threats against Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz by this evening

President Donald Trump was ratcheting up the tone against Iran early Tuesday ahead of his evening deadline to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

U.S. stocks were lower Tuesday, but still broadly positive in April, as President Donald Trump intensified his threats against Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz by this evening.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA was off 0.3% Tuesday afternoon, the S&P 500 index SPX was 0.2% lower and the Nasdaq Composite Index COMP was off 0.3% ahead of Trump's self-imposed 8 p.m. Eastern deadline for Tehran to reopen the crucial Middle East chokepoint to shipping and tanker traffic.

In March, the Dow and Nasdaq fell into a correction, defined as a drop of at least 10% from their prior closing high, but the S&P 500 narrowly avoided that big a drawdown following the U.S. and Israel's launch of attacks on Iran on Feb. 28.

"Tonight's deadline has been an overhang for markets early this week," said Anthony Saglimbene, chief market strategist at Ameriprise Financial. "But even if the U.S. were to attack tonight, I think the markets are hoping it would be measured, and the result would be Iran would come to the table - and maybe that speeds up a cease-fire."

Trump said "a whole civilization will die tonight," but then added: "WHO KNOWS?" in a social-media post Tuesday.

Fox News anchor Bret Baier, later on Tuesday, said he spoke with Trump, noting that, "8 p.m. is happening." But he also indicated the U.S. remained open to negotiations.

While traffic through the strait briefly appeared to pick up over the past few days, significant disruptions in the flow of oil and gas have been the norm since the war began, according to data from MarineTraffic.

Fresh jitters around the global oil shock had U.S. benchmark crude prices up another 2.5% to about $115 a barrel on Tuesday, while global Brent rose 0.8% to around $110 a barrel, according to FactSet. That reflects a surge of about 100% for U.S. crude and 81% for the global benchmark in 2026.

"The longer oil stays above $100 a barrel, the more damage it does to the U.S. economy and the global economy," Saglimbene said.

The Cboe Volatility Index VIX, known as Wall Street's "fear gauge," was pushing higher ahead of Trump's deadline, up about 9.9%, at last check, near 26.51.

"The market is certainly on edge as it relates to Iran," said Jed Ellerbroek, portfolio manager at Argent Capital Management. "The market is worried that as oil prices go up, that pushes up inflation more broadly, which is going to have a negative impact on GDP growth, and also the stock market, and also the [Federal Reserve's] ability to cut rates."

Until Tuesday's selloff, stocks had managed a bit of a rally despite a further climb in oil prices, suggesting some optimism that the surge in crude prices might be temporary. However, hedging activity tied to the S&P 500 has also remained robust, according to Joe Mazzola, head trading and derivatives strategist at Charles Schwab. "You are still seeing people going into this event pretty well hedged, and protecting against downside risks," he told MarketWatch.

This comes as the U.S. economy has been fairly strong and resilient, with corporate earnings still pegged to grow at a double-digit rate. "The market is trying to balance that really strong fundamental result with the big inflation and oil-price risk on the other hand," Ellerbroek said.

With that backdrop, hopes for a de-escalation around this evening's deadline is alive. "We have a playbook for this where we set hard deadlines and potentially use them as leverage points around negotiations," said James McCann, senior economist for investment strategy at Edward Jones.

"We've seen that in geopolitical policy and on trade policy, as well, from this administration," McCann said. "It's hard to say exactly what's in the price, but markets are looking for that as a potential way to alleviate some pressure from the situation."

The most likely scenario, in Ellerbroek's view, is that Trump finds a way to delay the U.S. military bombardment against Iran's infrastructure, a threat he has repeated. While Trump has demanded several times in the past few weeks that Iran reopen the strait, he's also pushed back his deadlines.

Should that happen again, Ellerbroek's "best guess" would be for a brief rally in stocks until the next deadline, but he also said it "feels like we're in kind of a never-ending loop with escalation."

Robert Schroeder contributed.

-Joy Wiltermuth -Frances Yue

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: LG Energy Reports 207.8 Billion Won Loss, Misses Estimates As EV Demand Weakens
data.description:
LG Energy Solution reported preliminary first-quarter results that may reinforce concerns around the EV cycle, with the company posting an operating loss of 207.8 billion won for the three months ended March 31, wider than the 140.5 billion won loss expected. Revenue declined 2.5% to 6.6 trillion won, while the company indicated that excluding US advanced manufacturing tax credits, the loss would have reached 397.5 billion won. The print suggests policy support is still playing a meaningful role in cushioning profitability. Shares closed 1% lower in Seoul ahead of the release, though the stock has still gained nearly 14% over the past month, potentially reflecting investor positioning around energy storage demand.

The pressure appears to be coming from a broad-based slowdown in EV momentum across key markets. In the US, policy changes under President Donald Trump, including the rollback of EV tax credits and fuel economy standards, have weighed on automakers and suppliers. General Motors flagged $6 billion in charges tied to production cuts, while Ford Motor outlined $19.5 billion in costs related to its EV overhaul and scrapped a 9.6 trillion won battery deal with LG alongside another venture involving SK Innovation. In Europe, Freudenberg Battery Power Systems canceled a 3.9 trillion won agreement as it exits the battery sector, underscoring the uncertain demand outlook facing battery suppliers.

Against that backdrop, LG Energy is pivoting capacity toward energy storage systems, where demand linked to AI-driven data centers could be emerging as a new growth driver. The company is converting multiple EV production lines to raise ESS cell output capacity to at least 60 gigawatt-hours from 36 GWh, while targeting at least 90 GWh in new orders this year. Some analysts suggest the first quarter could represent a cycle bottom, with expectations that operating margins may improve as ESS demand scales and the company leverages its early positioning in North America to support new contract momentum.

data.title: Germany's young men do not need permit for foreign travel, ministry said
data.description:
By Maria Martinez

Young men will continue to be able to travel abroad without prior permission despite Germany's new military service law, the defence ministry said on Tuesday, adding that it would issue a blanket exemption this week to avoid unnecessary bureaucracy.

The law includes a provision requiring men aged 17 to 45 to obtain Bundeswehr permission for stays abroad of more than three months, but the ministry said the rule was designed only for a "state of tension" and would apply only if military service became compulsory rather than voluntary, as it is now.

The law went into effect in January, but the requirement had gone mostly unnoticed until Friday, when it raised concerns.

The law was passed to boost Bundeswehr numbers and meet NATO targets amid the growing view within Germany that it has relied too long on the United States, and as tensions with Russia spur calls for stronger defence capabilities across Europe.

All 18-year-old men are now screened and asked by letter whether they wish to serve voluntarily. Women also receive such a letter, but unlike men, they are not required to respond.

data.title: Solana-based Stabble tells LPs to withdraw funds after identifying former North Korean employee
data.description:
Solana-based decentralized exchange Stabble went into apparent panic mode on Tuesday, urging liquidity providers to withdraw their funds in a series of emergency social media posts.

"EMERGENCY! Guys, please temporarily withdraw your liquidity instantly!" the DEX posted to X. "Better safe than sorry."

The rushed announcement appears to have been triggered by online sleuth ZachXBT, who just hours before, posted information about a North Korean developer having worked for years at Elemental, a Solana-based DeFi infrastructure project.

U.S. authorities have issued warnings about North Korean technology professionals using fake identities to infiltrate crypto companies. Additionally, over the weekend, Drift Protocol said its $280 million exploit was likely run by the same North Korea-aligned actors behind the Radiant Capital hack of October 2024.

Hours after ZachXBT's post about the Elemental employee, Stabble's team reposted the investigator's comments, which included a resume and photos of the alleged North Korean developer.

Stabble then repeated its warnings urging everyone to withdraw liquidity while adding, "This is the new team from Stabble, that aimed to repair the project. We will do new audits to be safe about our LPs. Then we can continue."

LPs, or liquidity providers, deposit their crypto on decentralized exchanges, helping to facilitate trading.

Stabble's DPRK employee

Commenting on Stabble's post, X user @jussy_world asked the DEX: "So, you have this North Korean employee working for you?"

To which Stabble replied: "It seems we had one year ago. We have a new team at Stabble that took over 4 weeks ago."

After several X users criticized Stabble's handling of the situation, the DEX posted that there had been no exploit and the warning messages were simply a precaution.

"There has been no exploit. We received a message and are acting on it. Our primary focus is the safety of our LPs," Stabble said. "We're not PR people, we're quants and early DeFi degens. We hear you, and your feedback matters."

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

data.title: Toll Brothers Announces Final Opportunity for Luxury Living at Toll Brothers at StoneCreek in Richmond, Texas
data.description:
Exclusive Richmond community offers elegant homes and resort-style amenities

RICHMOND, Texas, April 07, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Toll Brothers, Inc. , the nation’s leading builder of luxury homes, today announced the final opportunity for Houston-area home shoppers to purchase a new luxury home at Toll Brothers at StoneCreek, an exclusive community located in Richmond, Texas. Only a limited number of quick move-in homes remain available for sale in this desirable neighborhood.

Toll Brothers at StoneCreek brings luxury and sophistication to the StoneCreek Estates master plan. This community offers elegant home designs ranging from 2,941 to 3,813 square feet, with quick move-in homes from the mid-$600,000s. The homes are situated within a scenic, nature-inspired setting and feature move-in ready and quick move-in opportunities to suit any lifestyle.

Residents of Toll Brothers at StoneCreek will enjoy resort-style amenities, including a pool, splash pad, playground, walking trails, and tennis courts. The community is within walking distance of Don Carter Elementary School and is conveniently located near the shopping and dining destinations of Sugar Land Town Square. With easy access to Interstate 69 and less than an hour from downtown Houston, Toll Brothers at StoneCreek offers an ideal location for families and professionals alike.

"Toll Brothers at StoneCreek offers home shoppers the opportunity to live in a beautifully designed home within a vibrant, family-friendly community," said Brian Murray, Division President of Toll Brothers in Houston. "With limited homes remaining, this is truly the final chance to join this sought-after neighborhood."

The Sales Center is located at 5318 Gilded Estates Drive in Richmond. For more information, call 833-289-8655 or visit TollBrothers.com/Houston.

About Toll Brothers

Toll Brothers, Inc., a Fortune 500 Company, is the nation’s leading builder of luxury homes. The Company was founded in 1967 and became a public company in 1986 with common stock listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol “TOL.” Toll Brothers builds new homes and communities in over 60 markets across the United States, serving first-time, move-up, active-adult, and second-home buyers. The Company also operates its own architectural, engineering, mortgage, title, land development, smart home technology, landscape, and building components manufacturing businesses.

Toll Brothers was named the #1 Most Admired Home Builder in Fortune magazine’s 2026 list of the World’s Most Admired Companies®, the ninth year the Company has achieved this honor. Toll Brothers has also been named Builder of the Year by Builder magazine and is the first two-time recipient of Builder of the Year from Professional Builder magazine. For more information visit TollBrothers.com.

From Fortune, ©2026 Fortune Media IP Limited. All rights reserved. Used under license.

Contact:Andrea Meck | Toll Brothers, Senior Director, Public Relations & Social Media | 215-938-8169 | ameck@tollbrothers.com

A photo accompanying this announcement is available at https://www.globenewswire.com/NewsRoom/AttachmentNg/6307bcc0-6b5d-4f9b-b78c-10f1c5eb0020

Sent by Toll Brothers via Regional Globe Newswire (TOLL-REG)

data.title: Hightower to Fold $3.2 Billion Massachusetts Investment Advisory Practice Into New Centralized Channel — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kenneth Corbin

Hightower is expanding the East Coast footprint of its new centralized division with the addition of Lexington Wealth Management, a registered investment advisory firm based in Lexington, Mass., with $3.2 billion in assets under management. Lexington has been operating on Hightower's platform since the Chicago-based aggregator acquired it in 2019.

Hightower plans to incorporate Lexington into its Hightower Signature Wealth channel, or HTSW, a division the firm rolled out last fall. The Signature Wealth business is geared for advisory practices willing to cede control over some of their operations to their parent company, which sees the unit as an opportunity to consolidate some of the disparate advisory businesses it has been acquiring in recent years.

Signature Wealth marks something of a departure from what had been Hightower's main operating model: acquiring RIAs but allowing them to function with their own brands and a significant amount of independence.

Hightower has been working quickly to move firms it has acquired to the Signature Wealth channel, an effort complemented by external acquisitions. When the Lexington transition closes, Hightower says that Signature Wealth will operate in more than 25 locations with $29 billion in assets under management.

"Lexington is representative of the advisors we intentionally look to bring to HTSW — those with a clear client focus and a desire to grow within a more integrated model," says Hightower CEO Larry Restieri.

Lexington has a staff of 25, including 16 advisors. Hightower says it runs on a "collaborative, team-based model" that delivers "highly personalized" advice to its clients.

"We established Lexington on the principle that effective wealth management combines thoughtful financial guidance and a genuine understanding of what clients are experiencing in their lives," says Kristine Porcaro, a senior managing partner at Lexington.

As part of HTSW, Lexington will receive operational support from Hightower and access to a broad menu of investment options, with the back-office help aimed at freeing up advisors to focus more on their work with clients.

"One of the most important factors in this decision was ensuring long-term stability for our clients and employees," says Mike Tucci, a senior managing partner at Lexington, who says the new arrangement will strengthen his organization while preserving the "personalized, relationship-driven approach that defines our practice."

Write to advisor.editors@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: April Seasonality Signals 1.5% S&P 500 Gain As $20 Billion Flows Build
data.description:
April's seasonal bid is starting to re-enter the conversation just as US equities attempt to stabilize following a volatile stretch. Historically, the S&P 500 (SPY) has delivered an average gain of 1.5% in April since 1990, making it the second-strongest month of the year, and recent price action suggests a tentative recovery could be forming, with the index rising 0.4% and the Nasdaq 100 advancing 0.6%. This comes after a drawdown that pushed the S&P 500 more than 5% below its peak, and at one point as much as 9% below its January record, as geopolitical tensions tied to the Iran conflict triggered a sharp spike in oil prices and weighed on risk sentiment.

The underlying setup appears closely tied to retail liquidity dynamics around the US tax deadline. Investors often hold back capital ahead of Tax Day, then reallocate once filings are complete and refunds begin to flow back into accounts. Data from Barclays indicates the S&P 500 has gained an average of 0.83% between April 15 and month-end since 2006, while the Internal Revenue Service reports tax refunds are running 10% higher than the same period in 2025. That timing effect, combined with positioning shifts into earnings season, could help explain why buying activity tends to cluster in the second half of April, even if tax-related flows are not the sole driver.

Flows from systematic strategies may add another layer of support. Goldman Sachs' trading desk estimates roughly $20 billion could be deployed into US equities from commodity trading advisers and volatility-targeting strategies as prior selling pressure fades. At the same time, Vanda Research data suggests retail participation typically strengthens into the final weeks of the month after a softer start. With many investors having already reduced exposure during the recent geopolitical shock, the threshold for additional heavy selling may be relatively high, leaving room for a potential relief rally to develop into late April, particularly if tensions surrounding Iran begin to ease.

data.title: Oil Prices Rise After Kharg Island Strikes. Trump Says a Whole Civilization 'Will Die Tonight.' — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Callum Keown and Anita Hamilton

Oil prices were rising Tuesday as President Donald Trump ramped up threats against Iran with just hours remaining before his 8 p.m. Eastern time deadline for a peace deal that includes his demand for the full reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

The day began with attacks and threats as the U.S. carried out more than 50 strikes on military targets on Kharg Island, according to a White House official. Kharg Island handles 90% of Iran's oil exports.

Shortly after 8 a.m. Eastern, Trump wrote on Truth Social that a "whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again," unless Iran meets his demands.

"I don't want that to happen, but it probably will," he wrote, adding "maybe something revolutionary will happen," following a regime change.

Iran cut off direct communication with the U.S. following that post, The Wall Street Journal reported, citing Middle Eastern officials.

When asked Monday what it would take to call off the new assault that would include civilian infrastructure, Trump told reporters, "we have to have a deal that's acceptable to me and part of that deal is we want free traffic of oil."

Oil prices began heading higher Tuesday after Iran's Mehr News Agency reported the strikes on Kharg and Trump posted on Truth Social. Shortly after noon Eastern time, Brent crude futures — the international benchmark — had actually fallen slightly. But by mid-afternoon they were headed back up, rising 0.3% to $110.08.

Meanwhile West Texas Intermediate pared some of their earlier gains as well. After rising around 4% earlier in the day, they were trading up just 1% at $113.57 shortly after 2 p.m.

That's still shy of the $117.59 settlement prices hit in June 2022, when U.S. sanctions of Russian oil following its invasion of Ukraine pushed prices higher, according to Dow Jones Market Data. But any signal that strikes are imminent could send prices soaring.

The reason the U.S. benchmark is trading higher than international prices is because it is based on futures contracts for delivery in May, meaning it reflects how the market sees oil prices over the next four weeks, said Jaime Brito, executive director of Refining and Oil Products at Oil Price Information Service. Dow Jones is the parent company of both Barron's and OPIS.

By comparison, the price of Brent futures is based on contracts for delivery in June and reflects what the market expects over the next eight weeks.

Another factor keeping a relative lid on Brent prices may be the slight uptick in tankers successfully traversing the Strait of Hormuz over the weekend, according to ship tracking service MarineTraffic.

On Saturday, the number of crossings peaked at 11 — the most in a single day since March 1 — after Iran began allowing Iraqi vessels to move through the waterway. What's more there have been no reported physical attacks on ships there since April 1.

Trump said Monday that the U.S. could destroy all of Iran's bridges and power plants in just four hours if the country fails to agree to a deal and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

The U.S. Central Command said in a post on social-media platform X early Tuesday that U.S. forces continue to conduct strikes against military targets inside Iran.

An escalation would likely cause energy prices to climb further. Negotiators are pessimistic that Iran will meet Trump's demand to allow traffic to flow freely through the Strait of Hormuz, Journal reported.

The oil market's initial inactivity on Monday and early Tuesday suggested traders maybe don't believe Trump will hold to his deadline. After all, he has moved it several times over the past few weeks.

"Either there is a climbdown on the part of Washington or Tehran, which could prompt a major rally in equities and easing of energy prices, or a major escalation with all the implications that might have for financial markets," AJ Bell analyst Dan Coatsworth said.

There is a third outcome, though.

"An alternative scenario is that the deadline is extended, and the markets face another uneasy period of trying to gauge the latest mood music in the US and Iran," he added.

Write to Callum Keown at callum.keown@dowjones.com and Anita Hamilton at anita.hamilton@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Soybeans and corn turn lower on ample supplies; wheat mixed
data.description:
Key points:
  • Soybeans pressured by ample supplies, weak soymeal and soyoil futures
  • Wheat mixed as rain forecasts prompt inter-market spreads
  • Traders await Iran war updates and USDA crop report for further direction

By Julie Ingwersen

U.S. soybean and corn futures retreated on Tuesday on reminders of ample supplies, brokers said, but strength in crude oil underpinned the market as a standoff over the Strait of Hormuz threatened more escalation in the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.

Wheat futures were mixed as forecasts for much-needed rain this week in the U.S. Plains hard wheat belt prompted traders to sell K.C. hard wheat futures and buy Chicago Board of Trade soft wheat futures on inter-market spreads.

As of 12:54 p.m. CDT (1754 GMT), CBOT May soybeans (SK26) were down 8-3/4 cents at $11.58 per bushel and May corn (CK26) was down 4-3/4 cents at $4.49-1/4 a bushel.

CBOT May soft wheat (WK26) was up 3 cents at $5.98-1/4 a bushel while K.C. May hard wheat (KWK26) was down 1/2 cent at $6.07-3/4 a bushel.

SOY PRODUCTS DRAG DOWN SOYBEANS

Soybeans were pressured by declines in soymeal and soyoil futures. CBOT May soymeal (SMK26) was down about 1.6% and hit its lowest level in nearly three weeks as an aggressive U.S. soybean crushing pace generated ample supplies of soy products, including meal and oil.

"It's apparent that we are crushing for bean oil, and we are just creating excessive meal stocks," said Tom Fritz, a partner with EFG Group in Chicago.

CBOT soyoil futures also sagged, turning lower after the benchmark May contract (BOK26) set a life-of-contract high at 70.49 cents per pound.

Corn futures eased, with the nearby May and July (CN26) contracts at times falling more than new-crop December corn (CZ26) on spreads, a sign of soft cash markets amid plentiful U.S. and global stocks.

"We've got great demand, but the spreads are telling us that we've got more than enough corn to meet that demand," Fritz said.

CBOT wheat fell for a second day as forecasts of rain this week in a swathe of the U.S. Plains tempered concerns about drought stress to crops. The prospect of timely moisture muted the impact of lower-than-expected U.S. wheat condition ratings.

In its first weekly crop progress report of the 2026 growing season, the USDA on Monday rated just 35% of the winter wheat crop in good-to-excellent condition, a three-year low.

Corn planting was under way, with 3% of the expected area planted, the USDA also said.

Price moves were moderate as investors awaited Iran war developments, while crop traders were also looking ahead to a monthly U.S. Department of Agriculture crop report on Thursday.

data.title: Nigeria takes steps to plug oil revenue leaks, finance minister says
data.description:
By Camillus Eboh

Nigeria has begun enforcing a presidential executive order to plug revenue leaks, requiring state oil company NNPC and other agencies to deposit revenue to federal government accounts without deducting costs or fees, Finance Minister Wale Edun said on Tuesday.

  • Edun said a committee he chairs has stopped NNPC Ltd from taking a 30% management fee, with the funds now paid directly to the government.

  • The committee will issue clearer guidelines to ensure smooth, transparent remittance of all revenues due to all tiers of governments, without “extraneous deductions”.

  • Frontier exploration deductions and gas flare penalties are now remitted to the federation account, he said.

  • Nigeria is pushing to raise internal revenue through technology and digitisation, while weighing privatizations, divestments and public‑private partnerships.

data.title: Intel Joins Musk-Led Terafab Chip Venture
data.description:
Intel (INTC, Financials) is stepping into Elon Musk's Terafab chip venture, joining SpaceX, Tesla and xAI in an ambitious effort to reshape how advanced semiconductors are built.

The project is aimed at combining logic, memory and advanced packaging under one roof, with a goal of producing 1 terawatt of compute a year. Intel said its experience in designing, fabricating and packaging high-performance chips will help move that goal forward.

The partnership gives Intel a chance to tie itself more closely to one of the most closely watched AI and robotics initiatives in the market. It also fits with Intel's broader push to show that its manufacturing business can play a bigger role in the next wave of chip demand.

Terafab is expected to be developed in Austin, Texas, where two advanced chip factories are planned. For Musk's companies, the project is about gaining more control over semiconductor supply. For Intel, it is a chance to stay central to the AI hardware race.

Intel shares moved higher after the news, suggesting investors see strategic value in the partnership. The next catalyst will be whether Terafab can turn a bold vision into real production capacity.
